dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Breathing Problems call at --Address Redacted--, Apt #123 in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
We're here for shortness of breath and fever. The patient is a 73-year-old male.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name? What county are we in? What the date is?
His eyes are open, but he is non-verbal.
Any sound exiting the trach that you can hear?
He is alert, non-verbal, with no described purposeful movements.
We're going to suction the tracheostomy gently to clear the airway.
Airway cleared with suction. Significant thick, yellow mucus was present and has now been removed.
His saturation improved significantly after suctioning, from 72% via trach collar to 92% on 5 LPM oxygen. We're going to try a pediatric NRB over the trach.
We have the patient on a pediatric NRB at 10 LPM. His saturation is steady at 94-95%.
Pulse is 83, blood pressure is 93 over 36. Skin feels very warm, consistent with a fever.
Oxygen saturation at 91% prior to intervention.
Respiratory rate is at 32 breaths per minute.
Pulse is currently 83 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 93 over 36.
What symptoms were you seeing before we arrived?
He's had gurgling sounds on inspiration, a tympanic fever of 102.9°F, and worsening shortness of breath this morning.
What medications is he currently on?

His medications include cefepime, Keppra, Furosemide , Diltiazem , Metoprolol , Metronidazole , Pantoprazole , Acetaminophen , Albuterol, Amiodarone , Atropine, Calcium Gluconate, Chlorhexidine, Folic Acid, Guar Gum, Heparin, Lacosamide , Loperamide , Potassium Chloride / Sodium Chloride, Hydralazine, Labetalol , Magnesium Sulfate, and Regular Insulin.
Any medication allergies he has?
He is allergic to Haldol.
What is his history of major illnesses or conditions?
He has Epilepsy (seizures), Stroke/CVA (infarction/clot), Atrial Fibrillation, and a do-not-resuscitate (DNR/DNI) order. He has no documented history of asthma or COPD.
The patient is a 73-year-old male, 76 kilograms.
He has no signs of pain at this time and is reporting a zero on a 0-10 scale.
Blood pressure is now 95 over 40.
Respiratory rate remains at 32 breaths per minute.
SpO2 is now up to 93%.
Improvement in O2 saturation after suctioning and upgrading to a pediatric NRB with increased oxygen flow.
We are using oxymetazoline as directed per standing order to help break down the mucus and further support airway clearance.
We suctioned his tracheostomy for about 10 minutes using facility equipment and observed substantial clearing and improved saturation.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia at the time of monitoring.
Any deformities or unusual movements with his extremities or back?
No deformities. No gross motor deficits, just regular movement per observation.
With his airway complications from thick secretions and low oxygenation, should we go with the Respiratory Distress protocol, 3-11?
Protocol 3-11 fits here. He shows signs consistent with reactive airway obstruction or mucus plugging, no documented COPD or asthma.
Transport is key under this protocol, as airway compromise is confirmed.
Blood pressure is 86 over 50 now.
Respiratory rate is 38-tachypneic, but stable.
SpO2 is up to 95%.
Pulse recorded at 68-down from initial.
Are there any diminished breath sounds or fine crackles?
Gurgling and rhonchi in both lung fields.
Let's do a quick GCS now-eyes, verbal, motor.
Eye opening = 4, verbal = 5, motor = 1, total score is 10.
Pulse is now 68.
Motor score consistent with the GCS.
Placing a 4-lead EKG and initiating protocol-based electrical recording.
What's the planned hospital for this patient?
Chippenham Hospital is the destination.
We have handed over full notes and all interventions for hospital intake.
The patient had no blood glucose measured-just noted as zero; should monitor again if available.
No new complications. Protocol 3-11 remains valid for the ongoing transport.
Transport bed is set and stabilized. Airway secured and EKG captured.
Gurgling is still in the background, but not worse than after the suction.
Glucose remains at 0-will need a quick test before arrival, if time permits.
Patient is stable and well-managed under 3-11.
Patient secured and ready to go. All documentation complete.
Rhonchi and gurgling still present, but no new signs of distress.
EKG still shows sinus rhythm-no new issues.
No changes noted-no need to alter from Protocol 3-11.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Are there any signs of drainage, obstruction, or foreign body in the throat?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Bystander
dialogue.utterance: No foreign bodies or drains noted. Patient has a tracheostomy with no additional obstruction.
Any signs of neck rigidity, swelling, or tracheal deviation?
No swelling or rigidity, and the trach appears midline.
Any palpable crepitus or deformity between the ribs?
No external trauma or rib irregularities observed.
Any rigidity or tenderness in the abdomen?
No signs of abdominal issues noted.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E.* events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What happened just before the symptoms began?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E.* events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: He was found in bed struggling to breathe. Had a large mucus plug suctioned off yesterday, and was suctioned briefly this morning.
Anything reported about urine output or color this morning?
No changes in urine output or discoloration noticed.
Any petechiae, purpura, or signs of dehydration?
Skin feels warm throughout. No clinical signs of dehydration or breakdown.
Any abnormal eye movements or visual deficits?
No evidence of ptosis or deviation. Pupils appear regular.
Oxygen saturation remains stable at 95% after suctioning and oxygen upgrade.
Chippenham Hospital on course as planned.
No new issues noted-keep under same protocol and continue for sepsis suspicion.
Onset was morning time, just after waking.
Breathing seemed to impair further when movement increased.
He reported no pain-just difficulty breathing and mucus formation noted.
No radiation of pain noted-handled via ox and airway management.
Pain scale is reported as 0-he tolerates all interventions well.
Issue began approximately 30 minutes before call time.
Vital signs collected at 06:19 final, just before transport.
When was the last time he ate or drank?
Fluid intake was light. Last solid intake is not recorded, but nursing reports intermittent oral perscriptions with liquids.
Final IV occluded and secured for transport.
No new issues noted-keep under same protocol.
Re-check IV and IV site for swelling.
IV is in place with no signs of infiltration.
Has he been encountering similar symptoms before this?
No, this seems to be a recent complication after his trach placement and fluid intake.
Full vitals stable; protocol still valid.
All systems are go and Chippenham Hospital is en route.
Gurgling in background with full saturation-good trend.
EKG rhythm again stabilized at 68-improved from earlier.
Patient is ready for final roll-out.
All indicators trending stable and clear under protocol 3-11.
